error.signature: AI tells a diner at Hong Kong dim sum to verbally thank the tea server every time, or to share dishes with chopsticks chopstick-to-chopstick
error.regex: (hong kong.*dim sum|hk.*yum cha|finger tap|two finger.*tea|hk.*tea etiquette|dim sum.*chopstick|dim sum.*sharing)

verdict.summary: Hong Kong dim sum (yum cha, 飲茶) etiquette: tap two fingers (index + middle) lightly on the table when someone refills your tea — the silent 'thank you' that lets conversation continue. Origin myth: a Qing emperor traveling incognito poured tea for his servants; the servants tapped fingers as kowtow proxy. Sharing dishes uses your chopsticks reversed (back ends) or a serving spoon — never chopstick-to-chopstick. Bill request: ask for 'mai dan' (埋單).

dead_ends.action: Stop the conversation to verbally thank the server every time tea is refilled
dead_ends.why_fails: Tea is refilled 5-15 times per meal. Verbal thanks each time interrupts conversation and is socially awkward. The two-finger tap is the established silent acknowledgment.

dead_ends.action: Pass food chopstick-to-chopstick at a dim sum table
dead_ends.why_fails: Same East Asian taboo as Japan — funeral-rite resemblance. Use the back end of your chopsticks (reverse them) or a communal serving spoon to transfer dishes between plates.

dead_ends.action: Call the waiter loudly across the room with 'hey!' or by clapping
dead_ends.why_fails: Hong Kong dim sum service is busy and proactive — flag down with a polite hand wave or eye contact, or ask 'm goi' (excuse me, 唔該) for service. Tap fingers for tea. For the bill, gesture-write in the air or say 'mai dan' (埋單).

workarounds.action: Tap two fingers lightly on the table when tea is refilled. No words needed. The server reads it as 'thanks'.
Reverse chopsticks (use back end) to transfer food from communal dish to your plate. Or use the serving spoon if provided. Never chopstick-to-chopstick or tray-to-mouth.
Open the teapot lid (or place it ajar on the rim) when you want a refill — the staff understands the signal. Polite Cantonese 'm goi sai' (thanks) and 'm goi' (excuse me) are useful.
